comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around recent issues with Spotify playing ads for paid subscribers, with many users expressing frustration and some attributing it to bugs or mistakes. Several commenters mention switching to alternative services like YouTube or Jellyfin due to dissatisfaction with Spotify's policies and pricing.

comments.author: pythonaut_16
comments.text:
Seems like a bug. I had it happen (ads were playing and UI showed the premium upgrade nudges), then my Spotify refreshed and it went away again.

Annoying that it happened. Annoying that Reddit mods are aggressively removing the discussion. Annoying that HN comments here are immediately jumping to Spotify hate and the sky is falling.

Imagine if we all assumed every AWS outage meant that AWS was cancelled.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a plausible explanation that the ads might be a temporary bug, supported by personal experience, making it the most accurate and balanced perspective.

comments.author: vpribish
comments.text: IF this is happening it's gotta be a mistake.  Ads on paid spotify will be the end of my subscription.
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a reasonable assumption that ads for paid users would be a mistake, expressing a personal consequence, which aligns with typical user expectations.

comments.author: lschueller
comments.text:
Yeah.. They seem to speed up their entshittification game. I recently wrote a short piece about how Spotify is forcefully updating the app, and how to prevent it. For example, if you plan tonuse spicetify or something similar: https://duckass.bearblog.dev/how-spotify-silently-updates-it...

Spotify is losing ground after their last subscription fees increase, as far as I see it.
comments.analysis: This comment adds context about Spotify's aggressive app updates and pricing strategy, which is consistent with known user complaints, providing useful background.

comments.author: noemit
comments.text: I canceled my family subscription last month. We have Youtube Premium which I use to play music - Spotify was over 20/month and no longer made sense. We mostly used it to play music on our Google speakers for the kids. It's one of those products that used to make sense, but now just doesn't feel so critical. It's easy to replace and patch where I used it.
comments.analysis: A personal account of switching services due to cost and utility, reflecting a common consumer response to perceived value changes.

comments.author: Festro
comments.text: I gave up on Spotify as I started to listen to more podcasts which had their own ads inside them let alone Spotify's. Now I'm paying for Youtube (never thought I'd be doing that) and using the new(ish) jump ahead feature to skip in-video ad segments including in video podcasts. Problem largely solved?
comments.analysis: Provides a practical alternative and workaround for ads in podcasts, showing adaptability and user experience insight.

comments.author: sequin
comments.text: I left Spotify years ago. Youtube is so much nicer in terms of content alone. But Youtube, with its insane backlog of video's not available elsewhere, is straight up a monopoly, so they too will start squeezing customers at some point. In anticipation of that I've been collecting flacs again. It's actually kind of a nice hobby.
comments.analysis: Highlights broader industry concerns about monopolies and customer treatment, though somewhat speculative about future actions.

comments.author: cobertos
comments.text: I just migrated to Jellyfin and cancelled my Spotify subscription just last week (https://cobertos.com/blog/post/finally-cancelling-my-spotify). Paying off even more than I predicted. So sick of everything getting in the way of just listening to my music.
comments.analysis: Shares a concrete alternative to Spotify, reinforcing the trend of users seeking different platforms due to dissatisfaction.

comments.author: drcongo
comments.text: PSA: Stop using Spotify, they're predatory scum.
comments.analysis: An emotionally charged statement lacking evidence or nuance, reducing its credibility and constructive value.
